The whole segment enumerates the fields in which the addressee is 'accomplished'.
segments.s653-g187: زهر here is literal 'poison' used figuratively to modify the following phrase (a 'poisonous' or 'bitter' utterance). موبدی (موبدی) is the mōbed (Zoroastrian priest) in the oblique position as the source/agent, نوسخن is a compound 'new‑saying / new theme', and خواستی is the 2nd‑person singular past 'you desired/asked' — so the verb governs the whole noun phrase 'زهر موبدی نوسخن' as its object.
segments.s653-g188: دلش را is the object phrase: دل 'heart' + the enclitic pronominal ش 'his' and the object marker را, marking 'his heart' as the direct object. بدانش is a fused prepositional/relational form (بد + ان + ش) meaning 'with/through that (knowledge)' or 'to that knowledge', referring back to the preceding idea; بیاراستی is a classical/perfective verb meaning 'you adorned/arranged/set right' (a poetic past form of آراستن with a prefix) — so 'you set his heart with that knowledge.'
segments.s653-g189: بدانگاه is an adverbial phrase 'at that time / then'. نو is an adjective 'new / newly', and بود is the past copula 'was' (here used poetically). The name بوزرجمهر (Bozorgmehr) follows; the line uses a poetic inversion (predicate/adverbial first, then the copula and the subject name) common in classical Persian.
segments.s653-g190: This segment lists epithets attached to Bozorgmehr. سراینده is an agentive noun 'versifier/poet' (one who composes), وزیرک is a diminutive 'little vizier' (وزیر + diminutive -ک), وخوب is the conjunction و + خوب 'and good/well' (here 'and handsome/beautiful'), and چهر is 'face/appearance' — together giving 'a versifier, a minor vizier, and handsome of face.' Adjectival and appositive phrases are placed after the name in this poetic line.
segments.s653-g191: چنان is an adverb/adjective meaning 'so/thus' or 'such', introducing a characterization. بدکزان is a plural nominal form 'the bad/evil ones' and here qualifies the following nouns: موبدان 'mōbeds (priests)' and ردان (a poetic/archaic word here in apposition) — the phrase says 'such were the bad mōbeds and rādān'. The order places the qualifying word before the nouns it describes, a common classical pattern.
segments.s653-g192: ستاره شناسان is a compound noun 'star‑knowers' = 'astrologers/astronomers' (ستاره + شناسان 'those who know'), و هم is 'and also', and بخردان is a plural noun meaning 'the wise ones' (ب‑خرد‑ان, an honorific/plural formation). The segment appends two social/professional groups to the negative characterization begun in the previous segment.
segments.s653-g113: چوبشنید is the fused temporal clause چُو + شنید ‘when he heard’ (چو = when; شنید = past simple ‘heard’). دانا follows as the subject/predicative noun ‘the wise one’. ز is the poetic preposition ‘of/from’ introducing the thing heard about, and نوشین روان is a two‑word descriptive phrase (نوشین ‘pleasant/delightful’ modifying روان ‘flowing/fluent’), together meaning ‘the sweet‑/delightfully‑flowing (one)’. Word order is poetic (temporal clause → subject → prepositional phrase).
segments.s653-g114: سرش = سر + enclitic ش ‘his head’ (possessive enclitic). پرسخن is an adjective/compound ‘full of speech’ (پر/پرـ ‘full of’ + سخن ‘speech’) and گشت is the past tense of گشتن used as ‘became’. و links the clause to گویا زبان, where گویا here is an adjective ‘eloquent’ modifying زبان ‘tongue/speech’. Overall: ‘his head became talkative and his tongue (became) eloquent.’
segments.s653-g115: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase ‘gave such an answer’ (چنین ‘thus; so’ + داد ‘gave’ + پاسخ ‘answer’). که introduces the content clause that follows. در is the preposition ‘in’; خان here is an older/poetic locative noun meaning a dwelling/place associated with you (rendered here as ‘khan/house’); تو is the second‑person possessive ‘your’. The clause therefore begins the locative description: ‘that in your khan…’.
segments.s653-g116: میان is the locative preposition ‘among / in the midst of’. بتان is the plural of بت ‘idol’ (‘idols’). شبستان is a noun meaning ‘bedchamber, inner chamber, night‑room’ and تو is the possessive ‘your’. The phrase orders locatives poetically: ‘in the midst of your idols, (is) your bedchamber.’
segments.s653-g117: یکی is the indefinite ‘one / a certain’. مرد is the noun ‘man’. برناست is an adjectival/predicative form meaning ‘is (wearing) a berna/berna‑cloak’ (برنا = berna/cloak; -ست = archaic third‑person ‘is’). کز is the fused relative/preposition combination of که + ز used as ‘who / who from / who of’; خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive ‘himself / of his own’. Together: ‘a certain man, who of his own (will) …’.
segments.s653-g118: به + آرایش جامه is a prepositional phrase ‘with/into the adorned garment’ (به here marks manner/attire; آرایش ‘adornment/finery’; جامه ‘garment’). کرده‌ست is the compound perfect/past form ‘has made / has done’ (used here in the sense ‘has put on’). زن is the noun ‘woman’, so آرایش جامهِ زن = ‘a woman’s / feminine finery’. The whole segment means: ‘has dressed himself in women’s adornment.’
segments.s653-g119: ز is the preposition ‘from / away from’. بیگانه is ‘stranger(s) / outsiders’. پردخته is a past‑participle/adjectival form meaning ‘well‑made / well‑prepared / adorned’; کن is the imperative ‘do / make’. جایگاه is the object ‘place / spot’. Together the imperative instructs: ‘make/prep a place (set it apart) away from strangers.’
segments.s653-g120: برین is the compound of بر ‘on/for’ + the demonstrative/orthographic element ین (i.e. ‘on/for this’). رای ما = ‘our counsel/plan’ (رای here = plan, counsel). تا introduces purpose ‘so that / in order that’. نیابند is the third‑person plural subjunctive/negative form of یافتن ‘to find’ (here ‘may not find / do not find’). راه = ‘way / path’. So: ‘for this our plan, so that they find no way.’
segments.s653-g921: چنین داد پاسخ که = a reporting clause: چنین (thus/so) + داد (past of دادن, 'gave') + پاسخ ('answer'). The sequence is Subject‑Verb‑Object compressed into a formulaic 'thus gave answer that...', and که introduces the reported subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s653-g922: دل از عیب جستن ببایدت شست = the clause being reported. دل ('heart') is the theme; از + verbal noun جستن ('from/of seeking') forms a genitive/participial phrase 'of seeking fault'. ببایدت is an archaic/poetic second‑person obligation construction ('it is necessary for you' / 'you must'), and شست (root of شستن) is used here as the action 'to wash/cleanse' (infinitival/old poetic form), so grammatically 'you must wash the heart of fault‑seeking.'
segments.s653-g923: بی‌آهو کسی نیست اندر جهان = an existential statement. بی‑ (without) prefixes آهو to mean 'without an ahu'; کسی نیست is the existential 'there is no one' (کسی + نیست). اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' placed after the predicate, a typical poetic word order.
segments.s653-g924: چه در آشکار و چه اندر نهان = a correlative construction 'whether ... or ...'. The repeated چه introduces the two alternatives: در آشکار ('in the open / publicly') and اندر نهان ('in secret'). آشکار and نهان function adverbially/locatively here; اندر is the locative preposition.
segments.s653-g925: چومهتر بود بر تو رشک آوری = conditional protasis + result. چومهتر should be read as چو مهتر ('if/when he was greater/senior'); چو is the conditional 'if/when' and مهتر is comparative/superior. بود is the past copula. بر تو marks the relation/target (toward you / in relation to you) and رشک آوری is a person‑directed result: آوری (from آوردن) used here to mean 'produce/feel' (envy). Overall: 'If he were greater than you, you would feel envy (toward him).' (Note: the printed form compresses چو+مهتر into one token; parse it as two words.)
segments.s653-g926: چوکهتر بود زو سرشک آوری = the parallel conditional clause. چو کهتر بود = 'if/when he was smaller/inferior'; زو (archaic ز + او) = 'from him' or 'on his account'; سرشک ('tear, teardrop') + آوری ('you would bring/produce') = 'you would shed tears (for him)'. The verb آوری again expresses the resulting emotional response in this hypothetical frame.
segments.s653-g927: سه دیگر سخن‌چین و دوروی مرد = a noun phrase 'three other/remaining tattletales and two‑faced men'. سه دیگر = numeral + 'other'; سخن‌چین is a compound noun 'speech‑picker' = tattletale; دوروی is an adjective 'two‑faced' modifying مرد ('men/people').
segments.s653-g928: بران تا برانگیزد از آب گرد = purpose clause. بران is a directional/adverbial 'there/onto that place'; تا introduces purpose ('so that / in order that'); برانگیزد is subjunctive/optative (3rd person) 'may arouse/rouse up/drive out'; از آب گرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the water / out of the water' with گرد functioning as 'around/around it' or adverbially. Grammatically: 'send them there so that they may rouse it from the water.'
segments.s653-g993: چنین هم (literally “thus/also”) is an adverbial phrase: چنین = “thus/so,” هم = “also/too.” نگه‌دار here is an imperative/verbal idea meaning “keep/guard” (the lexical entry given is a noun, but in context it functions as a command or instruction). تن = “body” is the direct object; در + خورش = a small prepositional phrase ‘in/regarding its food’ (در = “in,” خورش = “food/dish”), so the whole segment reads “so also keep/guard the body with respect to its food.”
segments.s653-g994: نباید + که is an impersonal prohibition: نباید = “must not/should not,” and که introduces the subordinate clause. بگزایدت is formed with the old verbal prefix ب- + گزاید (choose/fit) + enclitic -ت (object “you” or optative). Here it has an optative/jussive sense “may (it) choose/fit you” (the poet uses it as something that must not happen). پرورش = “upbringing/education.” The clause thus means “it ought not to be that upbringing chooses you.”
segments.s653-g995: بخور is an imperative from خوردن “eat” (here used as an instruction). آن‌چنان = “in such a way/so,” and کان is an old/cohesive particle meaning something like “so that/inasmuch as” (classical usage). بنگزایدت again has the prefix ب- + گزاید + enclitic -ت: an optative/jussive construction meaning “may it befit/choose you” or “let it suit you.” Together: “Eat so (that) it may suit you.”
segments.s653-g996: ببیشی is the verbal/infinitive idea from بیشیدن ‘to increase / to give more’; here it functions as an instruction/imperative notion “take more / increase (the amount).” خورش = “food/dish,” تن = “body,” and بنفزایدت is b- + نفزاید + -ت, an optative/jussive: “may it increase you / may it augment your body.” So the clause says roughly “take more food so that it increases your body.”
segments.s653-g997: مکن is the prohibitive imperative “do not.” درخورش is the adjective درخور “appropriate/worthy” plus the clitic ـش “its,” so ‘in/for its appropriateness’ or ‘with respect to it’; خوی = “disposition/character” and را is the definite-object/topic marker. چار = “remedy / recourse” here likely means ‘a means’ or ‘recourse,’ and سوی = “toward/side.” The segment’s word order is poetic; literally: “Do not (make) your disposition be a recourse toward it (its food).”
segments.s653-g998: چنان خور = “eat thus/so” (چنان = “so,” خور here is the verb ‘eat’ used in the imperative or hortative sense). که introduces the result/cause clause “so that …”; نیزت is the cliticized/compound form meaning “to you again / also to you” (نیز = “also”), کند is the present/optative maker “does / makes,” and آرزوی = “desire/wish.” The clause reads “eat so that it makes/does not make you desire (it) again” — the poet uses this to warn against eating in a way that stirs renewed appetite.
segments.s653-g999: ز = poetic preposition “from/of.” می‌نیزهم is a compound adverbial (می + نیز + هم) meaning “also/even” or “as well” (the form here is emphatic). شادمانی = “joy/rejoicing,” and گزین is the imperative “choose.” So the segment reads “Choose joy even from wine” or “From wine as well, choose joy.”
segments.s653-g1000: که introduces an explanatory clause. مست = “drunk/intoxicated (one),” ازکسی = “from anyone” (often in negative contexts meaning ‘by anyone’), نشنود is the negative/present form “does not hear” (classical subjunctive/present), and آفرین here is a noun “praise/acclamation.” The clause thus explains the reason: “for a drunk hears no praise from anyone” (i.e., the intoxicated person cannot receive/listen to praise).
segments.s653-g585: چو + clause is a temporal subordinate: چو ... آید = “when ... comes.” سختیش is the noun سختی with the possessive clitic -ش (“his/its hardship”). پیش آید is a light verbal phrase (“comes forward/occurs”). از هر شمار is a prepositional phrase (“from/out of every count”) that links to the following predicate (it helps specify in what respect he becomes پیش).
segments.s653-g586: شود پیش is the main predicate following the temporal clause: شود = “becomes” and پیش here means “foremost/ahead.” و simply coordinates the next clause. سستی نیارد به کار is a negated result clause: نیارد (classical negative/causative form of آورد/نیاورد) functions here as ‘does not bring/enter,’ بهرِ کار expressed as به کار = “into the work/affairs.” Overall: ‘and slackness does not enter into his work.’
segments.s653-g587: ز نادان is a genitive/partitive phrase “of the ignorant.” که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (“which/that”), and گفتیم here is the narrator’s parenthetical ‘we said’ (classical narrative insertion) modifying the following phrase هفتست راه, which states the number of ways (هفتست = ‘seven’). Word order is compressed in poetic style but reads: “of the ignorant, which we said are seven ways.”
segments.s653-g588: یکی = “one” (one of those ways). آنک is a relative particle ‘that/which/who’ (classical form). خشم آورد is a verbal expression: آورد here = ‘causes/brings about’ (i.e. causes anger). بی‌گناه follows as the object/target: “the innocent” — so: “one is he who causes anger to the innocent.”
segments.s653-g589: گشاده کند is a causative/verbal phrase built from the adjective گشاده + کند = “(he) makes (it) open” → idiomatically “opens/gives freely.” گنج is the direct object “treasure.” بر + ناسزای = “toward/for the ungrateful” (بر here = ‘to/toward’); ناسزای is used substantively for ‘the ungrateful/insolent.’
segments.s653-g590: نه is the negative particle; ز او (here written as ز + و in the segmentation) = “from him/them.” مزد یابد = “does not obtain/receive reward” (یابد = obtain/attain). بهر = “for” and دو سرای = “two abodes/houses” (a classical reference to two worlds). The whole clause: “nor does he obtain reward from them for either of the two abodes.”
segments.s653-g591: سه دیگر = “three others.” به یزدان بود ناسپاس is a small clause: به یزدان = “toward God,” بود = past copula “were/were being,” ناسپاس = “ungrateful/thankless.” In short: “Three others were ungrateful to God.”
segments.s653-g592: تن خویش را is the object phrase: تن = “body,” خویش (here segmented as خوی + ش) = “one’s own,” and را is the definite/direct-object marker. در نهان is an adverbial “in secret,” and ناشناس is a predicative adjective “unknown/unrecognized.” The clause reads literally “(they) make/leave their own body in secret unknown,” i.e. “they are strangers to their own body in private.”
segments.s653-g353: چنینست is the fused copula + adverb (چنین است → 'thus it is'); رسم is the nominal head meaning 'rule/custom' and is followed by the two nouns قضا و قدر in coordination ('fate and destiny/decree') that specify the rule. Word order is copula-adverb + subject phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s653-g354: ز is the preposition 'by/through'; بخشش is the noun 'gift/grant'; نیابی is a negative present/future form of یافتن/یابیدن ('you do not/will not obtain' or 'you cannot attain') addressing the general second person; به + کوشش is an adverbial phrase 'by/through effort'; گذر here is a noun 'passing/advancement' (the result/means). The clause literally: 'you will not obtain by gift; [you must] pass by effort.'
segments.s653-g355: A sequence of epithets in apposition: جهاندار ('world-ruler/sovereign') + دانا ('wise') linked by و + پروردگار ('Lord/nurturer'). This is a nominal phrase listing divine titles; the final noun is the chief title.
segments.s653-g356: چنین is an adverb 'thus' placed before the verb آفرید (past tense 'created'); اختر روزگار is the direct object: اختر 'star' qualified by روزگار in a genitive/attributive sense ('star of time' or 'star of the ages', i.e. a marker of destiny). The verb precedes its object in this poetic word order.
segments.s653-g357: دگرگفت is a compound verb 'said otherwise / replied' (past); کان is an archaic demonstrative ('that' / 'such'); چیز is the subject 'thing'; کافزون is an adjective 'more abundant'; ترست is a contracted predicative form (تر + است → 'is more/greater'), common in classical/epic diction.
segments.s653-g358: کدامست is an interrogative 'which is it?'; و is the conjunction 'and'; بیشی is 'surplus/excess' or 'more'; the clause که را در خورست is relative/clarifying: که (relative 'which/that') + را (object marker attached to the relative) + در خورست (classical phrasing of درخور است, 'is fitting/worthy' — literally 'in-worthy is'). The order 'که را' (relative + را) is an older/poetic pattern marking the object of the relative clause.
segments.s653-g359: چنین گفت is a poetic inversion meaning 'thus spoke/said'; کان کس is the demonstrative + noun 'that person' or 'one'; که داننده‌تر is a relative clause 'who is more knowing' (داننده‌تر is the comparative form of 'knower'/'learned'). The whole segment introduces a speaker described as more learned.
segments.s653-g360: به نیکی is an adverbial phrase 'in/by goodness' (به used adverbially); کرا is an archaic relative/pronoun 'who/whom' (object/indeterminate); دانش آید is a present-tense clause 'knowledge comes' (دانش subject + آید 'comes'); ببر at the end is an imperative 'take!' — a command applying to the listener: 'take/learn (knowledge) from whoever goodness comes to.' The clause order is relative clause + imperative, typical of classical Persian exhortation.
segments.s653-g1017: هوا را مبر is a negative imperative: هوا (object) + را (definite/object marker) + مبر (do not take/bring). پیش رای و خرد places the preposition/relational word پیش (“before”) before the coordinated nouns رای و خرد (“counsel and wisdom”); و is the connective attached to خرد. The whole segment says ‘do not place whim/mood before counsel and wisdom.’
segments.s653-g1018: کزان پس begins a result/clause-of-consequence: کزان ‘from which’ + پس ‘afterwards/thereupon’. خرد is the subject (wisdom), سوی تو is a prepositional phrase (‘toward you’), and ننگرد is a negative present/optative form ‘does not regard/turn to’ (classical third person singular negative). The clause means ‘from that, afterward wisdom will not look to you.’
segments.s653-g1019: چوخواهی is a conditional/adverbial ‘if you wish’. که introduces the subordinate content clause ‘that ...’. رنج تو آید به بر is literal word order: رنج (suffering) + تو (your) + آید (comes) + به بر (upon; a poetic double prepositional phrase), so ‘that suffering come upon you’ (literal conditional meaning).
segments.s653-g1020: ز آموزگاران = ‘from teachers’ (ز = from). مپرتاب is a negative imperative (م‑ + پرتـاب ‹پرتاب› ‘do not cast/throw away’). سر (‘head’) is the direct object; the phrase idiomatically means ‘do not cast off/abandon teachers’ (do not spurn or throw away your relation to teachers).
segments.s653-g1021: دبیری بیاموز is an imperative: دبیری (the art/office of secretaryship) is the object and بیاموز is ‘teach!’ (classical imperative of آموختن). فرزند را uses را as the object marker after فرزند (the child), so ‘Teach the child secretaryship.’
segments.s653-g1022: چوهستی is the fused adverbial phrase چو هستی ‘as/according to his being’; بود is the copula ‘is/was’ (here linking the clause). خویش + ش is the possessive ‘his disposition/nature’ (ش = his), و joins پیوند را (‘and his kinship/lineage’ with را marking the phrase). Together the clause gives a proviso: ‘according to what his own nature and lineage are.’
segments.s653-g1023: دبیری رساند is a verbal phrase where دبیری (‘secretarial skill/office’) is the agent/topic and رساند (perfective/indicative ‘brings’/‘conveys’) is the verb. جوان را is the object (‘the young man’) marked by را, and به تخت is the destination ‘to the throne’. The line states that secretarial skill can raise a young man to the throne.
segments.s653-g1024: کند is the present/infinitive of ‘make/do’ (here ‘makes’). نا سزا (نا + سزا) is ‘the unworthy’ (نا‑ as negative prefix + سزا ‘desert/deserving’ used as noun). را is the object marker. سزاوار بخت is a predicate phrase ‘fit/deserving of fortune’ (سزاوار = deserving, بخت = fate/fortune). The clause reads ‘(it) makes the undeserving deserving of fortune.’
segments.s653-g1081: تو is the addressed pronoun 'you'. مپسند is a negative present/imperative form of پسندیدن meaning 'do not approve/choose' (classical negative prefix م-). فرزند را is the definite object (را marks the direct object) 'the son/child'. جای اوی is a noun phrase 'the place/stead of him' where جای has the sense 'instead/in the place of' and اوی is the archaic possessive 'his'. The clause literally: 'You do not choose the son in his place.'
segments.s653-g1082: چوجان (literary diminutive) = 'the little one/child'. دار here is an imperative or instruction 'keep/hold' (classical Persian دار from داشتن used imperatively), so the verb-like function is 'keep'. در دل is the locative prepositional phrase 'in the heart'. همه رای اوی is a genitive/possessive phrase 'all counsel/opinions his' meaning 'let all counsel be his' or 'make every decision/support his'. Word order follows classical Persian: verb (دار) with preceding object/locatives.
segments.s653-g1083: به شهری introduces a locative/directional phrase 'in/to a city' (به + شهری). که هست is a relative clause marker + copula 'which/where is'. اندرو is an older form meaning 'in it/within it' (reinforcing location inside that city). مهرشاه is a name or title (here treated as a proper name/title). The whole clause: 'in a city where Mehrshah is (present)'.
segments.s653-g1084: نیابد is a third-person form of یافتن (here 'does not find' or 'is not found'); نیابد is often used in classical Persian to mean 'one does not find'. نیاز is the noun 'need/want'. اندر ان (written اندران) = 'therein/among them'. بوم راه literally 'land/realm (بوم) path/way (راه)' — idiomatically 'in that land' or 'in that country'. Together: 'one does not find need therein in that land.'
segments.s653-g1085: بدی 'evil/wrong' is the noun subject/topic. از تو 'from you' marks the source of that evil. از فر او combines از + فر + او meaning 'from his farr (divine/royal glory)' — the preposition + possessed noun. بگذرد is a third-person present/subjunctive/indicative of گذشتن 'to pass' used here to mean 'passes/goes away' or 'is pardoned'. The clause indicates that any badness coming from you will pass with respect to his farr.
segments.s653-g1086: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because'. بختش 'his fortune/fate' is the topic (possessive -ش). همه نیکویی 'all goodness' is the object phrase (همه = all; نیکویی = goodness/virtue). پرورد is a classical perfect/past form of پروردن 'to nurture/bring up', here 'has nurtured' or 'has fostered'. Together: 'for his fate has fostered every goodness.'
segments.s653-g1087: جهان را uses را as a topicalizer ('as for the world'). دل is the true subject 'the heart' (i.e. people's heart/joy). ازشاه 'from the king' or 'because of the king' gives the cause. خندان بود is the predicate 'was smiling/joyful' (خندان = smiling; بود = was). Word order is topicalized noun + subject + circumstantial + predicate, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s653-g1088: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. بر چهر او is a locative expression 'on his face' (بر = on; چهر = face; او = his). فر یزدان is a genitive/compound 'the farr (glory) of Yazdan' — 'divine radiance/glory'. بود is the past copula 'was'. So: 'for on his face was the divine radiance.'
segments.s653-g833: This is a verbless predication built around the noun+copula phrase 'آزادیست' (آزادی + -ست = 'it is (a) nobility/freedom'). 'به' here preposes the predicate and functions adverbially ('with/regarding'), 'از خرد' uses از to mark source or grounding ('by/from wisdom'), and 'هرکسی' is the indefinite subject ('anyone') placed at the end as is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s653-g834: چنانچون introduces a simile ('just as/as when'); ببالد is a present/formal verbal form (classical -د ending) meaning 'be proud' or 'takes pride' (here third-person); ز + اختر = 'because of/from a star' (star = fortune/lot); بسی modifies اختر as 'abundant/many' (poetic 'much/enough'). Together: 'just as one is proud because his star is abundant.'
segments.s653-g835: دلت is دل + second-person possessive suffix (-ت) = 'your heart'. مگسل is a prohibitive/negative form (م + گسل) meaning 'do not split/break'. ای is the vocative particle 'O', and شاه راد is the vocative epithet 'king, generous' (راد used as an epithet). از خرد here gives the cause/topic ('about/over wisdom' or 'on account of wisdom').
segments.s653-g836: This clause treats خرد ('wisdom') as the grammatical subject and پرورد as a finite verb meaning 'nurtured/has fostered' (archaic past/perfective form). نام و فرجام را is the definite object (را marks the definite accusative) meaning 'name (reputation) and outcome/destiny'. So literally: 'Wisdom nurtured (or nurtures) name and fate.'
segments.s653-g837: منش پست و کم دانش is a nominal phrase ('low/basely-minded and little-learned') that qualifies the person introduced by آنکس ('that person/the one') and the relative clause introduced by که ('who/that'). گفْت here functions nominally/predicatively as 'said' (the one 'who said' — i.e. introducing quoted speech or a saying).
segments.s653-g838: This is the quoted boastful utterance. کنَم is an archaic/poetic first‑person verbal form ('I do / I am'), followed by کم ز گیتی ('one of the few in the world' — کم used partitive/quantificationally + ز گیتی), and کسی نیست جفت is an existential clause 'there is no one who is a match' (کسی = anyone, نیست = does not exist, جفت = match/pair). Grammatically, Persian places the negative/existential predicate after the noun phrase.
segments.s653-g839: چنین گفت is the formulaic 'thus said/so he spoke' (چنین = thus, گفت = said); پس is temporal/continuative 'then/after that'; یزدگرد دبیر is the nominative name + title 'Yazdegerd the scribe', the speaker identified after the formulaic phrase.
segments.s653-g840: که‌ای here introduces the direct speech that follows (که + the vocative ای). شاه دانا و دانش‌پذیر is the vocative address 'O king, wise and receptive (to learning)'; دانا and دانش‌پذیر are coordinate epithets describing the king.
segments.s653-g857: This clause reads like a temporal/conditional remark: 'چوکژی' is a compact poetic form (here treated as 'when [a] wrong [was] done') and is not one of the supplied senses, so it's marked -1. 'کند' is the verb 'does/makes' (sense 0) and stands before the noun phrase; 'پیر' is the noun 'old man/elder' (sense 0); 'ناخوش بود' is a predicative adjective + past copula meaning 'was unwell/ill at ease' (ناخوش sense 0, بود as past 'was' sense 1). Note the classical word order where the descriptive predicate follows the subject and the past copula 'بود' closes the clause.
segments.s653-g858: 'پس' here functions as 'after' (sense 1) introducing the temporal consequence; 'ازمرگ' literally 'from death' (sense 0) = 'after his death'; 'جانش' is the possessive 'his soul/life' (sense 0); 'پرآتش' is an adjectival compound 'full of fire/aflame' (sense 0); and 'بود' is the past copula 'was' (sense 1). The clause therefore states a past condition of his soul after death.
segments.s653-g859: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when' (sense 1). 'کاهل' is the adjective 'lazy/idle' (sense 0). 'بود' is the past copula 'was' (sense 1). 'مرد' here means 'man/person' (sense 0) and is modified by 'برنا' 'youthful/young' (sense 0). 'به کار' is a prepositional phrase 'at/in (his) work' — 'به' used as 'towards/into/at' (sense 1) + 'کار' 'work' (sense 0). The clause states a past condition (the young man being idle in his duties).
segments.s653-g860: 'ازو' is the prepositional pronoun 'from him' (sense 0). 'سیر' functions here as the predicate idea 'sated/tired' (sense 0). 'گردد' (from 'گشت/گرد') is a classical/poetic present/subjunctive form used as 'becomes' (sense 0). 'دل' is 'heart' (sense 0) and 'روزگار' here has the sense 'Fate/time' (sense 1); together 'دل روزگار' is 'the heart of Time/Fate' and the clause means 'the heart of Fate becomes sated/tired of him.' Note the verb ('گردد') follows the object phrase in the poetic order.
segments.s653-g861: 'نماند' is the negative past form 'did not remain/was not left' (sense 0). The preposition 'ز' (sense 0) plus the negative prefix 'نا' (sense 0) form 'ز نا تندرستی' — 'from lack of health' or idiomatically 'no health remained'. 'تندرستی' is 'health/well‑being' (sense 0) and 'جوان' is 'the young (man/person)' (sense 0). The clause literally says 'no health remained from the young (one).' The compact order and the negative prefix are typical of classical Persian.
segments.s653-g862: 'مبادش' is the optative/negative wish 'may it not be for him' (sense 0), a conventional poetic curse/wish form. 'توان' here is the noun 'strength/ability' (sense 1). 'و' links the two wishes (sense 0). The repeated 'مبادش' again negates the following noun, and 'روان' is 'soul/spirit' (sense 0). So the formulaic line prays/declares that he have neither strength nor spirit.
segments.s653-g863: 'چو' is 'when' (sense 1). 'بوزرجمهر' is the proper name Bozorgmehr (sense 0). 'این سخنهای' is 'these words/sayings' (این 'this' sense 0, سخنهای plural 'sayings/speeches' sense 0). 'نغز' is the adjective 'pithy/witty/delicate' qualifying the sayings (sense 0). Word order places the temporal clause first, then the actor and his object.
segments.s653-g864: 'شنید' is the simple past 'he heard' (sense 0). 'و' links the actions (sense 0). 'بدانش' is a fused prepositional/ pronominal form here meaning 'with/to that (them)' — i.e. 'by/with those [sayings]' (sense 1: 'to that'). 'بیاراست' is the past form 'he adorned/arrayed' (sense 0) and 'مغز' is 'brain/mind' (sense 0). The clause means he took those sayings into his mind — 'he adorned his mind with them.' Note the object ('مغز') follows the verb in this poetic line while the clitic in 'بدانش' points back to the earlier 'سخنها'.
segments.s653-g777: چواین = چو + این, a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' (here 'when these...'). گفتها is the plural 'these sayings/words' (گفتة with plural/ demonstrative). بشنود is a classical narrative-form present (subjunctive/indicative form used like a past in epic narrative) meaning 'he heard'. پارسا is a noun 'the pious one' and functions as the person who performs the hearing; word order places the temporal clause before the subject.
segments.s653-g778: خرد راکند is written fused here but parses as خرد + را + کند: 'خرد' = 'wisdom' (direct object), 'را' marks the object, and 'کند' (classical present) = 'puts/makes/sets'. بر دلش is a locative phrase 'upon his heart' where -ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'. پادشا at the end is a vocative or addressee 'O king' (classical usage often places the vocative after the clause).
segments.s653-g779: کند is the verb 'makes/does' (classical present used narratively). آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation/praise' (not the verb stem). تاج is 'crown' and برشهریار is a prepositional phrase 'upon/to the king' (بر + شهریار) indicating the beneficiary/recipient of the crown.
segments.s653-g780: شود is the copular/auxiliary 'becomes'. تخت شاهی = 'the throne of kingship' (تخت = throne, شاهی = kingship). برو in this older text functions as a fused locution (classically 'بر او' or similar) meaning 'upon it/thereon' rather than a literal 'go'; پایدار is an adjective 'lasting/steady'. Together: 'the royal throne becomes lasting.'
segments.s653-g781: بنازد is a present-form verb meaning roughly 'is proud/boasts' or 'is renowned' (classical idiom). بدو = 'to him' (دِ + او), pointing the pride toward the person. تاج شاهی و تخت lists the things that 'boast' or are 'proud'—'the crown of kingship and the throne'—with و linking the two nouns.
segments.s653-g782: بداندیش is the subject adjective/noun 'ill‑wishing / malicious (person)'. نومید is a predicate adjective 'hopeless' and گردد (classical present of گشتن) = 'becomes'. زبخت is an adverbial/agentive phrase 'by fortune' or 'because of fate' (classical phrasing). The clause means that ill‑wishing people are driven to despair by fate/luck.
segments.s653-g783: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگردد is the verb 'turns back/returns' (classical present used for a future/recurring event). این چرخ ناپایدار = 'this unstable wheel'—here 'چرخ' is the metaphorical 'wheel (of fortune)' and ناپایدار qualifies it as 'unstable/transient'.
segments.s653-g784: ازو = 'from/of him' (prepositional/enclitic form), نام نیکو = 'a good name' where نام carries the sense 'reputation/fame' and نیکو = 'good'. بود is the copula (past/neutral) 'was/will be' in classical narrative, and یادگار is the predicate 'a memorial/lasting remembrance'—together: 'a good name of him was/remained as a memorial.'
segments.s653-g17: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بنشست is the simple past 'sat down'. می‌خوردن is the verbal noun/infinitive form 'to eat' (here used with the sense of the act of eating being begun or arranged). آراستی is a perfect/preterite form meaning 'arranged/made ready' and links to می‌خوردن: the eating was put in order/prepared as he sat.
segments.s653-g18: وزان is a compound prepositional phrase 'and/from that', pointing back to something just mentioned. جام is the noun 'cup' (the container). نوشین‌روان is an adjective compound describing the cup/drink as 'sweet‑flowing' or 'sweet‑souled'. خواستی is a past tense of خواستن 'desired/wished' (3rd‑person narrative here), so the whole segment says he desired that cup.
segments.s653-g19: چوخورشید functions as 'when the sun' (چو = when + خورشید = sun). برزد is an archaic past verb meaning here 'came/issued/rose forth' (poetic). سر 'head' is used metaphorically for the sun's upper part, and از برج گاو locates that motion 'from the (zodiac) sign/tower of the Bull' (برج گاو = Taurus).
segments.s653-g20: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = from, هر = every, سو = side/toward). برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. خروش is 'a roar/bellow', and چگاو is a simile 'like a cow' — together: 'a bellowing like a cow arose from every side.'
segments.s653-g21: نشست is the simple past 'sat' (here a finite verb). از بر تخت کسری is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'on/upon the throne of Khosrow' (تخت = throne, کسری = Kisra/Khosrow), and دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' describing the sitter. Word order is inverted for poetic effect (adjective after the phrase).
segments.s653-g22: ازان دیده is an ablative phrase 'from/at that sight' (ازان = from that; دیده = sight/eye). گشته is the perfect/ resultive 'has become/was turned', and دلش پر ز غم states the resulting condition: 'his heart (دلش) is full (پر) of grief (غم)'. The clause links the cause (that sight) to the inner effect (heart full of sorrow).
segments.s653-g23: گزارنده خواب is a noun phrase 'the reporter/narrator of the dream' (گزارنده modifying خواب). را is the definite object marker. خواندند is the past plural 'they called/summoned' (here transitive): 'they summoned the dream‑narrator.'
segments.s653-g24: This short clause has an obscure noun ردان (here left untranslated as a specific group/title in the manuscript tradition) followed by the object marker را. ابر گاه is a poetic locution — literally 'cloud‑place' — used here as 'the lofty/appointed place or hall', and بنشاندند is the past plural 'they seated/installed'. The clause therefore says 'they seated (the) ردان in the high/appointed place.' (Note: ردان is an archaic/obscure word in this context; its precise sense is uncertain.)
segments.s653-g73: بدادش is an archaic past construction: ب‑ + داد + ـش (the enclitic ـش = 'to him'/'him'), functioning as the main past verb 'gave (to him)'. فرستاده here follows and works as a participial/verbsense element 'sent' (or can be read as 'a sent [item]'); اسب و درم are the direct objects ('horse and dirhams') listed after the verb. Word order is Verb + (participial/verb) + objects, typical in older Persian narrative.
segments.s653-g74: دگر here means 'other / additional', and هرچ is an indefinite relative 'whatever'. بایستش is bāyast (an old modal/impersonal past sense) with enclitic ـش = 'for him' meaning 'was necessary/needed for him'. از بیش و کم is an idiomatic phrase 'with regard to more and less' = 'whether more or less'; the preposition از links the quantity phrase to بایستش.
segments.s653-g75: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they departed/went'. هر دو ('each two') functions idiomatically as 'both (of them)', and برابر here means 'side by side/ together' or 'equally' — so 'they both went together/from Merv'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and مرو is the place name 'Merv' (not the negative verb meaning 'do not go').
segments.s653-g76: خرامان is an adverbial participle meaning 'striding/proudly' (a manner adverb). چو introduces a comparison 'as/like', followed by the image 'زیر گل' (زیر = 'under/beneath', گل = 'flower'), and اندر links the simile into the verb phrase (in/within). تذرو is a poetic present-infinitive/verb meaning 'to scatter' or 'to trample/scatter' — together the phrase paints 'striding proudly as if scattering/trampling under the flowers.'
segments.s653-g77: چنان هم is 'thus/so also' (چنان = 'so/thus', هم = 'also/too'), introducing that same manner applied to the following actors. گرازان is the plural 'boars' (here likely a troop of boars or boar-hunters), و links to گویان 'the speakers/proclaimers' (here a participial/agent noun meaning 'those who call/say'). ز شاه is the source/authority 'from the king'.
segments.s653-g78: This is a piled list of sources/reasons introduced by ز 'from': فرمان 'command/order' is first. وز here is an archaic linking particle repeated between items (not the meteorological sense), and فر is 'far' meaning royal radiance/glory; the next وز repeats the linker; تاج 'crown' and و گاه 'and time/occasion' close the list. Grammatically, the sequence lists the royal grounds/attributes (command, glory, crown, occasion) associated with the preceding actors.
segments.s653-g79: رسیدند is the past plural 'they arrived/reached'. جایی کجا is a doubled locative expression 'a place where' (جایی = 'some place', کجا = 'where'), followed by آب 'water' as the substantive located there. بود is the past copula 'there was', completing the relative clause 'a place where there was water.'
segments.s653-g80: چو here functions temporal/comparative 'when'/'as', introducing the circumstance. هنگامه in this context means 'the moment/occasion' (i.e., the proper time), خوردن 'eating' and خواب 'sleep' are nouns/masdars, and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'when it was the time for eating and for sleep.'
segments.s653-g1161: ز دریا is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea' placed before the rest of the clause (poetic word order). یکی ریگ is the indefinite subject 'one pebble' and دارد is a present-tense third-person singular 'has' (possession). به کف is a locative phrase 'into / in the palm' describing where the pebble is held.
segments.s653-g1162: دگر is anaphoric 'another/other (one)', with the subject omitted and understood to be 'one (pebble)'. دربیابد is a third-person present verb 'finds' (poetic/older form of رسیدن/یافتن), and میان صدف is a locative 'in the middle of a shell'. The clause therefore states that another pebble is found inside a shell.
segments.s653-g1163: جهان زنده بادا is an optative wish: جهان 'the world' + زنده 'alive' + بادا (optative particle) = 'may the world live'. بنوشین‌روان is a vocative/imperative cluster (benushin + ravān) addressed to the soul: 'drink, O soul' (a poetic exhortation).
segments.s653-g1164: همیشه is an adverb 'always'; به فرمانش is a prepositional phrase 'by/at his command' (به + فرمان + enclitic -ش 'his'), specifying agency; کیوان روان is a noun + adjective 'Saturn (کیوان) moving/flowing (روان)' — here روان describes the celestial body as in motion, so the whole clause means 'may Saturn ever move at his command.'
segments.s653-g1165: نگه کرد کسری is the verb+subject pattern 'Khosrow kept/turned his gaze' (کرد used as the light verb with نگه). بگفتا راوی is older word order where the verb 'بگفتا' ('he said') appears before its subject راوی 'the narrator' — literally 'the narrator said.'
segments.s653-g1166: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic -ش 'his'); گشت is the past / resultative 'became' and خرم is the predicate adjective 'joyful'. به دیدار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'at the sight/meeting of him' where اوی is an archaic/poetic form of 'او' with enclitic force; together: 'his heart became joyful on seeing him.'
segments.s653-g1167: چو here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when'. گفتی that follows is the verb form 'you said' in everyday Persian but the provided sense list does not match this verbal use (sense marked -1). که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause. زه بدره بودی is an older phrasing meaning roughly 'you were (of) the full moon' (بدره = full moon) and بودی is past 'you were'; some elements (like زه) are archaic or idiomatic here and are assigned -1 where the listed senses do not fit.
segments.s653-g1168: بدین گونه is the fixed phrase 'in this manner / thus' (here split into prefix and parts in the word list, but functioning together as an adverbial). بد بخشش: بخشش is 'gift / grant' (here 'bestowal of gifts'); بد in the list normally means 'bad', but in this collocation the precise nuance is archaic/poetic and does not match the straightforward 'bad' sense (sense set to -1). شهریار is the noun 'the king' and is the agent of the verb implied here: 'the king bestowed gifts in this way.'
segments.s653-g713: ز یزدان (literally 'from God') precedes the verb as a prepositional/agent phrase meaning 'of God' or 'God‑fearing'; بترسد is a third‑person present/jussive form with the b‑prefix expressing obligation/optative — 'let him fear' or 'he should fear'; گه داوری is a temporal clause 'when (it is) judgment' where گه + verbal noun داوری ('judging'/'judgment') gives 'when judging'.
segments.s653-g714: نگردد is a negative jussive/present meaning 'should not become' or 'must not turn into'; به میل is an adverbial phrase 'according to (one's) desire'; و links a parallel phrase; بکنداوری is a verbal noun/compound added in parallel to 'به میل' ('and by ...') describing another improper basis for judgment. Together: 'let [judgment] not become according to desire and by ...'.
segments.s653-g715: خرد راکند is a verbal phrase in which راکند (literally 'uprooted/dug up') acts on خرد ('wisdom') — here used figuratively 'uproots/destroys wisdom'; پادشا is the subject ('the king') appearing after the verb (inverted order), and بر هوا is a causal/adverbial phrase 'on/through whim' indicating the motive.
segments.s653-g716: بدانگه 'thereupon' / 'in that case' links the result; که introduces the following clause; خشم آورد پادشا is verb + object + subject order ('brought anger the king' = 'the king became angry') with آورد used causatively/bring‑about — 'the king brought forth/was filled with anger'.
segments.s653-g717: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it ought not / should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause; اندیشه شهریار is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the thought of the king' (noun + possessor). The whole clause means 'it should not be that the king's thought ...'.
segments.s653-g718: بود is the copula 'be' (here in the sense 'be the case'); جز is the restrictive preposition 'except'; پسندیده is a participial adjective 'pleasing/approved'; کردگار is 'the Creator/God', so پسندیده کردگار = 'that which pleases the Creator'. The phrase as a whole: 'should not be except that which pleases God.'
segments.s653-g719: ز یزدان here marks the divine agent ('God') and shenasad is a present verb 'knows/recognizes'; همه خوب و زشت is the object 'all the good and the bad' with همه quantifying the following adjectives. Word order places the agental phrase before the verb in classical style: 'God knows all good and bad.'
segments.s653-g720: به پاداش نیکی is a prepositional phrase 'for the reward of goodness/good deeds'; بجوید is a general present 'one seeks' or 'he seeks' with an implicit subject; بهشت is the object 'paradise'. Literally: 'for the reward of good (deeds) one seeks paradise.'
segments.s653-g145: The clause begins with the indefinite determiner 'یکی' (a certain) + 'دختری' (maiden), producing 'a certain maiden.' 'مهتر' here functions as a descriptive title/adjective (literally “chief” or “distinguished”) placed after the noun, and 'چاج' is a proper name. 'بود' is the past copula 'was' that completes the nominal identification ('there was...').
segments.s653-g146: 'به بالای سرو' is a locative prepositional phrase: 'به' marks location/position (here 'at/on'), followed by 'بالای' ('on top of') and 'سرو' ('cypress'). The coordinating 'و' links a second nominal phrase 'ببر عاج' (literally 'tiger of ivory'); 'بود' again is the past copula applying to the whole description, so loosely 'and an ivory tiger was (there).'
segments.s653-g147: This noun phrase describes the slave: 'غلامی' (a slave/youth) is modified by two compound descriptors: 'سمن پیکر' (literally 'jasmine-bodied' — i.e. with a jasmine-like figure or beauty) and 'مشک‌بوی' ('musk-scented'). The adjectives follow the noun in classical/poetic order, forming a compact descriptive cluster.
segments.s653-g148: 'به' introduces the locative phrase 'به خان پدر مهربان' — 'at/to the khan (court/house) of the kind father'; 'خان پدر مهربان' is a genitive sequence (title/house + father + adjective). The final two words 'بد بدوی' are poetic/elliptical: here they function as a terse description of origin — 'بد' as ‘ill/poorly’ and 'بدوی' as 'original/primitive' — together conveying 'of poor or ill birth.' Note that the verb 'بود' is not repeated; the past copula from the previous clause is understood.
segments.s653-g149: 'بسان' is a simile marker meaning 'like' followed by 'یکی بنده' ('a certain servant') — together 'like a servant.' 'در پیش اوی' means 'in her presence' or 'before her': 'در' (in), 'پیش' ('in the presence of'), and 'اوی' is the archaic/poetic third-person pronoun referring back to the woman. Word order is typical poetic Persian: simile + noun + locative + pronoun.
segments.s653-g150: 'به هر جا که رفتی' is a standard relative clause 'wherever she went' ('به هر جا' = 'to whatever place', 'که' = relativizer, 'رفتی' = past 'went'). The following nominal phrase 'بدی خویش اوی' lacks an explicit copula (poetic ellipsis): 'بدی' = 'evil/badness', 'خویش' = 'her own disposition', and 'ش اوی' (written as the enclitic 'ش' + 'اوی') marks possession/emphasis ('her'). So the clause reads roughly 'wherever she went, her own bad disposition (was apparent).'
segments.s653-g151: 'بپرسید' is the past tense verb 'asked' (third-person past). The short preposition 'ز' (from/about) introduces the object of inquiry. The conjunction 'و' links the next speech-act verb. The following items 'گفت کین مرد کیست' are reported speech: 'گفت' would normally be 'said' (here the provided senses list lacks the spoken-verb sense), 'کین' is the demonstrative 'this' (an archaic form written 'کین' rather than 'این'), and 'مرد کیست' = 'who is the man?' The copula 'ست' (is) completes the question.
segments.s653-g152: This is a direct question: 'کسی کو چنین بنده پرورد کیست' — 'کسی' = 'someone/person', 'کو' here is an archaic interrogative/relative particle ('who/whom/which') and the sequence 'چنین بنده پرورد' means 'who nurtured/raised such a servant' ('چنین' = 'such', 'بنده' = 'servant', 'پرورد' = verbal stem 'nurtured/raised'). 'کیست' is the copula-question 'who is (it)?' Note: some individual sense entries provided in the word list are archaic or do not exactly match the grammatical use here; for such mismatches I marked the sense index as -1 above.
segments.s653-g1001: چو is the conditional subordinating conjunction 'if'. یزدان is the subject 'God'. پسندی here expresses 'approves' (an older/poetic present form). پسندیده‌ای is formed from the past participle پسندیده 'approved/pleasing' plus the archaic 2nd-person copular ending -ای, giving 'you are approved/pleasing'. The clause reads 'If God approves, you are approved.'
segments.s653-g1002: جهان is the topic 'the world'. چون + تنست is a simile/categorizing clause: چون 'like/as' + تنست (تن + -ست) meaning 'is a body'—an older copular construction attaching -ست to the noun. و links the two clauses. تو چون دیده‌ای uses تو 'thou/you' + چون 'as/like' + دیده (noun 'eye') + -ای (the 2nd-person copula), i.e. 'and you are like an eye' or 'you are the eye' of the world.
segments.s653-g1003: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/of great measure' (classical usage). از introduces the source/target 'of' (here 'of the world‑creator'). جهان‌آفرین is a compound 'world‑creator' (the Creator). یاد کن is the imperative 'remember' (یاد + کن). The clause orders to 'remember much the Creator of the world.'
segments.s653-g1004: پرستش is the noun 'worship' (direct object). برین is an adjectival/superlative form meaning 'foremost/highest' (here modifying یاد). یاد بنیاد کن is a causative-imperative phrase 'make/establish the remembrance' (یاد + بنیاد + کن = 'set the remembrance as a foundation'). Together: 'Set the remembrance of supreme worship as a foundation.'
segments.s653-g1005: به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase 'with depth / profoundly / attentively' (به + ژرفی). نگهدار is an imperative 'keep/guard' (classical present imperative). هنگام را is the definite object 'the hour/the time' (هنگام + را as object marker). The clause means 'Guard the time with deep attention.'
segments.s653-g1006: به‌روز و به شب = 'by day and by night' (به‌روز 'of the day' and به شب 'by night'). گاه آرام را = 'the time/place of calm' with را marking the object. The whole clause instructs to keep both day- and night‑periods peaceful or at rest.
segments.s653-g1007: چودانی is an address 'as you know' (classical phrase, lit. 'you know that'), introducing a reminder. که is the subordinator 'that'. هستی here means 'being/existence'. سرشته is a past participle 'fashioned/created' (from سرشتن). ز خاک = 'from earth/dust' (poetic z for از). The clause: 'As you know, that your being was fashioned from earth.'
segments.s653-g1008: فرامش مکن is a negative imperative: فرامش (forgetfulness/forget) + مکن 'do not' → 'do not forget'. راه یزدان پاک = 'the way/path of the pure God' (راه 'path', یزدان 'God', پاک 'pure' as adjective). The full clause warns 'Do not forget the path of the pure God.'
segments.s653-g105: برفتم is a past first-person verb meaning “I departed/left” in an archaic/poetic form; the literal modern would be برگشتم/رفتم, so treat it as the main past action. ز is the preposition “from” placed before its noun phrase; درگاه شاها is a genitive/vocative phrase: درگاه (court/presence) + شاها (vocative, “O king!”). به مرو is a following prepositional phrase “to Merv,” with به indicating direction.
segments.s653-g106: بگشتم is an archaic past first-person verb form meaning “I returned/turned (back).” چو functions as the temporal conjunction “when.” اندر + گلستان is a locative phrase “in/within the rose‑garden.” تذرو is here a verbal/lexical element meaning “(it) scattered / was dispersed” used poetically as the predicate of the temporal clause; the subject is implicit.
segments.s653-g107: ز is the preposition “from.” فرهنگیان is a plural noun meaning “the cultured/learned people” (here the source group). کودکی is an object noun meaning “a child” (the thing found). یافتم is the past first-person verb “I found.” The order puts the source (from the learned) before the verb, a normal poetic variation.
segments.s653-g108: بیاوردم is past first-person “I brought (him).” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” تیز functions adverbially here as “swift(ly).” بشتافتم is another past first-person verb “I hastened/ran.” The two past verbs form a short sequence of actions: I brought him and then hurried.
segments.s653-g109: بگفت is a third-person past “he said/uttered” (the b- prefix is a poetic/archaic marker). آن سخن is the direct object “those/that words.” کزلب او is a prepositional phrase meaning “from his lips” (کز = از + ک poetic clitic), with او as the possessor. شنید is the past verb of the relative clause “he heard,” so the whole clause reads “he said the words which he had heard from his lips.”
segments.s653-g110: ز here introduces the topic/source “about / concerning.” مار سیاه is the noun phrase “the black serpent/snake.” آن شگفتی is “that wonder/marvel.” که دید is a relative clause “which he saw” (که = which/that; دید = he saw). The whole segment therefore links the spoken words to their subject: “about that black serpent — the wonder that he saw.”
segments.s653-g111: جهاندار is a title meaning “the world‑lord/sovereign.” کسری names the sovereign (Khosrow) in apposition. ورا is the pronominal object “to him” (ور + ا). پیش here marks the position “before/in the presence (of the king).” خواند is a third-person past verb which in this collocation means “summoned/called.” Together: “The sovereign Khosrow summoned him before [him].”
segments.s653-g112: وزان is a compound of و + ز + آن meaning “and from that” (linking back to the dream). خواب here is “the dream.” چندی is an adverbial expression “for a while / for some time.” سخنها is the plural “words/tales.” براند is a poetic form from راندن used here to mean “he put forth/uttered” (i.e. he related/uttered words). The clause therefore reads “and from that dream he spoke words for a while.”
segments.s653-g57: عنانرا is عنان + the direct-object marker -را, so “the reins” as the object. بپیچید is a past narrative form (ب‑ + پیچید) meaning “(he) turned/whirled”; the subject موبد follows the verb in this word order (verb + subject). ز راه is a prepositional phrase “from the road” placed here at the end of the segment but syntactically attaches to the coming motion verb (بیامد) in the next segment.
segments.s653-g58: بیامد is the past tense “(he) came.” بپرسید is another past-narrative verb (ب‑ + پرسید) “(he) asked.” The sequence shows two successive actions: he came and then asked. ز + (و) here mark the object of پرسید: ز means “from/of,” and the following clitic (written here as و) represents the pronoun ‘him’ (زو = ز + او ‘from him’). خواب شاه is a possessive/genitive phrase “the king’s dream” (خوابِ شاه) and is the content of the question.
segments.s653-g59: نویسنده گفت is 'the scribe/author said' (here نویسنده = the author/scribe; گفت functions as the speech verb introducing direct speech). این نه کارمنست is an archaic fused construction: این = “this,” نه = negation “not,” and کارمنست = کارِ من است (“is my work/my doing” with the copula fused). Together: “This is not my doing.”
segments.s653-g60: زهر دانشی is a noun phrase where دانشی (knowledge) qualifies زهر: “the poison/venom of knowledge.” زند is a substantive here placed after that phrase (in older usage it can refer to the Zand/commentary or appear as a name), and یارمنست is the fused form of یارِ من است (“is my companion/ally”) — an older compound for the copula. The whole clause says that the (figurative) venom of learning/Zand is his companion.
segments.s653-g61: ز موبد is a prepositional phrase “from the mobed.” چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بشنید is the past tense “(he) heard.” The subject follows the verb: بوزرجمهر (Bozorgmehr). Word order here is prepositional phrase + conjunction + verb + subject: “When Bozorgmehr heard from the mobed ...”.
segments.s653-g62: بدو داد گوش literally “gave ear to him”: بدو = to him, داد = gave (past), گوش = ear/attention — an idiom meaning he paid attention to him. و = “and,” بر + افروخت is a prefixed verb meaning “kindled/brightened” (lit. ‘kindled upon’), and چهر = face. Together: “he gave him his ear and his face lit up.”
segments.s653-g63: باستاد here is an older/formal verb phrase meaning “he stood up/rose” (in context it introduces his reply); گفت is the speech verb “said.” این شکارمنست is “this is my prey/quarry” where شکارمنست = شکارِ من است (an idiomatic claim of ownership or specialty: “this is my field/specialty”).
segments.s653-g64: گزاریدن خواب is an infinitival/verbal noun construction: گزاریدن here means “to relate/interpret” (to report or render), and خواب = “dream.” کارمنست is, as above, کارِ من است (“is my work/my task”). So the clause states: “Interpreting dreams is my job.”
segments.s653-g961: دگر = 'another' (qualifies دانش). دانش آنست = 'knowledge is that' (آن + ست is the copular construction 'that is'). کز begins a relative/causal clause 'that/from which'; here it introduces the source ('from/through what is eaten'). خوردنی is a substantive/masdar meaning 'what is eaten' or 'food'.
segments.s653-g962: فراز آوری جمعِ معنایی: فراز (height, elevation) + آوری (bringing) = 'bringing up/raising'. روی آوردنی is a verbal-nominal phrase from روی آوردن 'to turn toward/face' (‑نی nominalizes), so here it names the effect: 'raising and turning (toward)'. Both -نی forms are noun-like gerunds describing what the food produces.
segments.s653-g963: بخورد و بپوشش are exhortative/imperative-style commands formed with the poetic prefix ب‑ plus the present stem (here used as 'Eat!' and 'Put on / Clothe yourself'). the enclitic -ش on بپوشش refers to 'it/yourself' (clothing). به یزدان گرای: گرای is an older/poetic form meaning 'turn (toward)' or 'be inclined'; together it means 'turn toward God' or 'be God‑inclined'.
segments.s653-g964: بدین here functions as 'with this / in this (matter)' (بـ‑ as prefix + دین/این in older orthography), دار is a noun 'abode/place' used to indicate locus ('in this place/house'); فرمان یزدان = 'the command/decree of God'; به جای = 'in place / in its proper place'. The clause is elliptic (no overt verb), giving an instruction: keep/hold God's command in this place/rightful position.
segments.s653-g965: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. آیدت is composed of آید 'comes' plus the clitic ت 'to you' = 'comes to you / befalls you'. روزی here is adverbial 'one day / ever' (if ever). به چیزی نیاز = 'in need of something' (به + direct object + the noun نیاز 'need').
segments.s653-g966: This is a coordinate list of locations/things introduced by repeated به 'to/at': به دشت (the plain), و به گنج (and the treasure), و به پیلان مناز (and the elephants' dwelling/station). مناز is a noun 'dwelling/station' modifying پیلان (elephants); the construction warns against despising or neglecting those sources—the verb governing this list is elliptical and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s653-g967: هم از پیشه‌ها = 'also/from the professions' (هم 'also', از 'from'). آن گزین is an imperative 'choose that one'. کاندروی is an archaic/contracted phrase (literally 'که اندر وی' → 'which in it' or 'by which') linking this object of choice to the following clause; it functions as a relative phrase.
segments.s653-g968: ز نامش = 'from/of his (or one's) name/reputation' (ز = 'from/because of', نام + ـش = his/its name). نگردد is the negative jussive/future of گشتن 'to become' (ن‑ negation + گردد/becomes) meaning 'will not become'. نهان = 'hidden'; آبروی = 'honor/reputation' — the clause means 'so that by/through his name his honor will not be hidden' (i.e., choose a trade that preserves your reputation).
segments.s653-g153: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' that sets the manner. برگزیدی is the 2nd‑person singular past tense 'you chose' (classical word order often places the verb before its object). دلیر و جوان form the direct object, two adjectives coordinated with و; the noun they modify (a man) is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s653-g154: میان is a preposition meaning 'in the middle of / amid'. شبستان is the locational noun 'bedchamber / inner chamber' and نوشین‌روان is an epithet/name in the genitival/attributive relation (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe), so the phrase means 'in the middle of Nushin‑ravan's bedchamber'.
segments.s653-g155: چنین گفت زن is a literary inversion: the adverb 'thus' + past verb 'said' followed by the subject 'the woman' (i.e. 'the woman said thus'). کین here is a demonstrative referring to the youth ('this one'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from/than' used in comparison; کهترست is the copular predicate 'is lesser/inferior' where -ست is the classical 3rd‑person copula.
segments.s653-g156: جوانست is a predicate with -ست (classical 'is') meaning 'he is young'. و links the next clause. با من here functions like 'with me / as for me' (i.e. 'in relation to me'), ز یک مادرست asserts 'is from one (the same) mother' — یک مادرست = 'of one mother' with the copula -ست again, so the clause says they share the same mother.
segments.s653-g157: چنین جامه پوشید is 'she clothed/garmented herself thus' (past tense, 3rd singular; classical Persian often omits the explicit subject). کز is the fused form of 'که + ز' meaning 'because / from which' and introduces the reason: شرم شاه = 'shame/modesty before the king' (reason for her action).
segments.s653-g158: نیارست کردن به رویش نگاه is a clausal complement expressing what she did not do: نیارست is a negative/limiting form meaning 'did not bring herself / did not dare / did not manage' (classical usage), کردن is the light verb used to build the verbal phrase, and به رویش نگاه = 'to (his) face a look' = 'to look at his face' (به + روی + -ش 'his' + نگاه 'gaze').
segments.s653-g159: برادر‌گر is a conditional phrase: برادر ('brother') + گر ('if' / 'were'), so 'if (he is) a brother' or 'as a brother'. از تو = 'from you' (i.e. turning his face away from you). بپوشید here is the past/actional sense 'he covered/veiled (his face)' and روی is the object 'face'. The clause expresses the hypothetical action of hiding his face toward you.
segments.s653-g160: ز شرم تو بود آن بهانه inverts the normal word order: 'that excuse was from/because of your shame' (ز introduces the cause, شرم تو 'your shame', بود is the past copula 'was', and آن بهانه 'that pretext'). مجوی is a prohibitive imperative 'do not seek' (classical negative jussive), addressing the addressee and telling them not to look for another excuse.
segments.s653-g785: بماناد is an archaic/poetic third‑person optative/subjunctive (بمانَد) meaning 'may he remain'—the main desiderative verb. تا introduces a temporal clause 'until/while'; روز is the time noun 'day'; باشد is a subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used inside the تا‑clause ('while there be / while it is day'). جوان (postposed) is the predicative noun 'the youth' that completes the clause ('while the day exists, the youth [may remain]').
segments.s653-g786: هنر یافته is a compound in which یافته (past participle) modifies هنر to mean 'skill‑acquired' or 'accomplished in art'—an adjectival phrase describing the person. جان here is the poetic noun 'soul' (used as an epithet), and نوشین‌روان is a compound adjective 'sweet‑souled' that further characterizes that soul/person. Together they form an appositive description of the جوان mentioned earlier.
segments.s653-g787: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by/because of' and introduces the cause. گفتار او means 'his speech/words' (گفتار governed by ز). انجمن is the subject 'the assembly'; خیره شد is the verbal phrase 'became astonished/stunned' (خیره = astonished, شد = became). Word order is cause → object → result, typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s653-g788: همه رای (literally 'all counsel/opinions') has همه as a quantifier modifying رای. دانندگان follows as the modifier 'of the knowers/wise men' (post‑nominal attributive). تیره شد is a predicate meaning 'became dark/ominous/confounded'—i.e. their deliberations were clouded. شد is the past/result verb 'became'.
segments.s653-g789: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نوشین‌روان is the subject ('the sweet‑souled' man). آن سخنها is the object 'those words' (آن = that/those, سخنها = plural 'words/speeches'). شنود is an archaic poetic form of 'he heard' (third‑person past/remote form of شنیدن commonly used in epic narration).
segments.s653-g790: به روزیش is a dative phrase 'to/for his day/fortune' (به + روز + possessive ش). چندانک is an adverbial of degree 'to such an extent/so much'. بد is the quality 'bad/ill'. برفزود is an archaic past form ('he increased' / 'he made more of') from فزودن with the prefix بر‑; together the clause means 'he so increased the badness of his fortune' → 'he greatly increased his misfortune.'
segments.s653-g791: وزان is a fusion of و + زان meaning 'and from that / from those', linking this result to the previous clause. پندها = 'those counsels/admonitions'. دیده پر آب کرد is a causative/perfective construction: دیده ('eye') + پر ('full (of)') + آب ('water/tears') + کرد ('made/did') — literally 'made the eye full of water', i.e. 'filled his eyes with tears.'
segments.s653-g792: دهانش = 'his mouth' (دهان + possessive suffix ش). پر از ... کرد repeats the 'made full of' pattern: پر ('full') + از ('of/from') + در (here functioning as a locative/idiomatic connector 'in' or 'with') + خوشاب ('pleasant water/drink') + کرد ('made'). The net meaning: 'he filled his mouth with pleasant/drinkable water' (poetically 'his mouth was filled with pleasant drink').
segments.s653-g689: برو here is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle meaning something like “then/at once” (not an imperative). خواندند is past 3rd‑person plural “they recited/sang.” آفرین is the word they voiced — an acclamation (“Afarin,” “bravo/praise”), functioning as the object/content of خواندند. موبدان is the plural subject/agent “the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests),” placed after the verb for metrical/poetic order.
segments.s653-g690: کنارنگ functions adverbially/relationally as “beside/alongside.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بیداردل is a compound adjective “wakeful‑hearted” (vigilant, emotionally alert). بخردان here is the plural noun with a prefixed b‑ (poetic shortening of با) meaning “with the wise / the prudent,” so the whole phrase describes who stood alongside and in what disposition.
segments.s653-g691: ستودند is past 3rd‑person plural “they praised.” شاه جهان را is the definite object phrase “the king of the world” (شاه modified by جهان; را marks it as the definite object). بسی is an adverb meaning “much/greatly,” placed after the object for emphasis — overall: “they praised the king of the world greatly.”
segments.s653-g692: برفتند is past 3rd‑person plural “they departed/went away.” با is the preposition “with.” خرمی is a noun of manner — “mirth/delight” — describing how they left. هرکسی is “each person / everyone,” indicating that every individual departed in that manner; word order places the manner phrase before the person for poetic rhythm.
segments.s653-g693: دوهفته is the duration noun “two weeks.” بر + ین together represent برِ این (poetic fusion) meaning “on/for this (matter/time).” نیز is “also”/“too,” and بگذشت is the intransitive past “passed” (time passed). شاه appears after the clause as the topical/next subject introduced for what follows; overall: “Two weeks on this also passed.”
segments.s653-g694: بپردخت (بپرداخت) is past 3rd‑person singular/polite — here “he dealt with / attended to.” روزی is used adverbially “one day / on a day” (poetic placement before the clause). ز is the poetic form of از “from/of.” کاری سپاه literally “the work/affairs of the army” — together: “one day he attended to the army’s business.”
segments.s653-g695: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate purposive/command clause “that / so that.” موبدان و ردان are the coordinated plural nouns — “the mobeds and the rdan” — the addressees of the command. The clause introduced by تا reports what he ordered them to do.
segments.s653-g696: به is the preposition “to/into.” ایوان is the destination “iwan / porch/veranda.” خرامند is a plural present‑participle/adverbial participle meaning “striding proudly / swaggering,” describing how they should enter. با is “with,” and بخردان (poetic fusion of با + خردان) means “with the wise/prudent,” indicating the company in which they enter.
segments.s653-g745: Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: سپهبد (the commander) is the subject, followed by به فرهنگ which indicates the manner or standard (literally 'by/with culture/discipline'), دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/keeps', and سپاه is the object 'army'. In modern prose you would expect سپهبد سپاه را به فرهنگ دارد; here the object is placed at the end for rhythm.
segments.s653-g746: براساید is a present infinitival/impersonal verb meaning 'it befits' or 'is fitting'; the phrase از درد 'from/out of pain' gives the cause, and فریادخواه is the person described ('one who cries for help' or petitioner). The clause is impersonal/axiomatic: 'it is fitting for the petitioner to cry out from pain.'
segments.s653-g747: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'; آژیر 'alarm, shrill signal' is the thing present; باشی is a subjunctive/present form of بودن here functioning like 'there is / is sounded' or 'be' in the time clause; ز دشمن 'from/by the enemy' indicates source; برای at the end of the segment is an adverbial 'on account of / because of' which connects to what follows. Word order is poetic and compact.
segments.s653-g748: بداندیش ('the ill‑minded' or malicious person) is marked by the object/topic marker را; دل 'the heart' is the grammatical subject of برآید, a present/subjunctive verb meaning 'rises/starts to move' (literally 'comes up'); ز جای 'out of its place' gives the location from which the heart moves. The clause describes a startled heart reacting to the alarm.
segments.s653-g749: همه 'every/all' modifies رخنه 'breach'; پادشاهی 'of the kingdom' is a genitive modifier. بمرد is an optative/exclamatory form literally 'may (it) die' or 'let it perish'—a kind of curse or wish applied to the breaches. The clause is short and forceful as a wartime admonition.
segments.s653-g750: بداری here is a present/imperative sense of 'have/hold/keep' (an instruction to maintain or guard); به هنگام is a fixed adverbial 'at the time of' and پیش از نبرد 'before the battle' completes the temporal phrase. Together this gives an order about readiness just before fighting.
segments.s653-g751: به چیزی 'about/for something' begins the conditional/relative idea introduced by که 'that'; گردد is a subjunctive/present of شدن 'become/happen'; نکوهیده is a predicate adjective 'censured/blamed' describing the thing; شاه 'the king' is the agent whose disapproval is meant. Grammatically this is a relative/conditional clause: 'concerning anything that becomes censured by the king.'
segments.s653-g752: نکوهش 'reproach/censure' is the noun; بود is the copula 'is/was' (here general present sense 'there is'); نیز 'also' links this to what follows; با فر و گاه literally 'with glory and time/occasion'—با + فر attaches the idea of reproach occurring together with royal splendour or in certain occasions. The clause states that censure accompanies (or affects) even royal glory and occasions.
segments.s653-g1025: دبیریست is a nominal predicate formed from دبیری + the old copular -ست (equivalent to است) meaning 'being/office of scribe is'. از پیشه‌ها is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the professions' following the predicate (Persian often places the predicate before its complements in poetry). ارجمند is an adjective 'esteemed/valuable' that modifies the implied subject (دبیری).
segments.s653-g1026: کزو = 'from which / because of which' links this clause to the previous one (i.e. 'by/through which'). مرد افگنده is a noun + participle ('a man cast down/fallen') where افگنده is a past participle of افکندن. گردد بلند is a copular + predicate: گردد (گرد + آید/گردد) 'becomes/is made' and بلند 'high/lofty' — together 'is raised high'.
segments.s653-g1027: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. با آلت و رای is a coordinated complement 'with instrument (tools) and counsel (advice)'. باشد is the present/subjunctive copula here meaning 'has/is provided with' (poetic use of باشد after the complements). دبیر is the subject 'the scribe' and follows the verb phrase in this word order.
segments.s653-g1028: نشیند is a present/subjunctive form of نشستن 'to sit' used here as a modal/result verb 'will/shall sit' (poetic usage). بر پادشا is a prepositional phrase 'before/upon the king' (بر + پادشا). ناگزیر functions as an adverb/adjectival phrase 'inevitably/compelled', indicating that the action (sitting before the king) is unavoidable.
segments.s653-g1029: تن خویش is a possessive sequence: تن 'body' + خوی + ش where خوی + ش (the suffix ش = 'his') yields 'his own body' (an older, split orthography of خویش). آژیر دارد literally 'has an alarm/cry' (آژیر functioning as a vocal or alarm image) and ز رنج is the causal/genitive phrase 'from suffering' explaining the source of the alarm.
segments.s653-g1030: بیابد is a present indicative 'he finds/obtains' (here 'receives' from the king). بی‑اندازه is an adverb 'immeasurably/excessively' modifying the verb. از شاه گنج is a prepositional phrase 'from the king (شاه) [a] treasure (گنج)' — 'treasure from the king.'
segments.s653-g1031: بلاغت is the subject 'eloquence'. چو 'when/if' introduces the clause. با خط 'with (one's) script/handwriting' is an instrumental/comitative phrase. گرد آیدش is a compound verbal idea: گرد (part of a light-verb or circumstantial particle) + آید with the enclitic ش 'to him/it' — together 'comes to it / attaches to it' (i.e. eloquence attaches to the handwriting).
segments.s653-g1032: براندیشه is a prepositional compound 'بر + اندیشه' meaning 'upon/upon thought' (target of the addition). معنی is the direct object 'meaning'. بیفزایدش is a present tense verb بیفزاید 'adds / increases' with the enclitic ش 'it' (referring back to معنی) — 'it adds meaning to thought'.
segments.s653-g177: همی ... فزون بود is a poetic, progressive phrase: همی (an adverb) gives a continuous sense and precedes the time-word to mean “day by day” or “continually.” روز روزش is a repetitive/possessive sequence (روز + روزش) that yields the idea of his days/time; فزون (adjective/participial) goes with بود (past copula) to mean “was increasing” or “grew.” بخت is the subject (fortune, luck). Word order in Persian poetry can be inverted for rhythm: literal sequence is ‘continually day his-day increasing was fortune.’
segments.s653-g178: This short clause contains two coordinated ideas compressed without an explicit conjunction: بدو شادمان = ‘to him (it was) joyful’ (بدو is the prepositional dative 'to him/for him' and شادمان is a predicative adjective meaning 'happy'). بد دل شاه سخت is a noun‑adjective phrase describing the king: بد دل = 'ill‑hearted / bad‑hearted' (بد modifying دل) and شاه سخت = 'the king [was] severe/harsh' (سخت as predicate). The copula 'was' is implied or carried from the previous clause.
segments.s653-g179: دل شاه کسری is a genitival noun phrase 'the heart of Khosrow the king' (دل + [title + name]). پر از داد بود = 'was full of justice': پر از (full of) + داد (abstract noun 'justice') + بود (past copula 'was'). The order places the possessed noun first (heart) and the possessor after it in the emphatic poetic word order.
segments.s653-g180: به دانش دل ومغزش آباد بود uses به (here meaning 'with' or 'by means of') to mark the instrument/cause: 'by knowledge.' دل و مغزش = 'his heart and his marrow' (ومغزش is 'and his marrow' fused by the conjunction‑prefix و). آباد بود is a fixed predicate meaning 'was flourishing/prosperous/enriched.' Thus the whole clause says his inner being was prosperous through knowledge. Note و attached to مغزش is ordinary Persian clitic‑coordination.
segments.s653-g181: بدرگاه بر موبدان داشتی: بدرگاه 'in/at the royal court' is the locative phrase; بر here functions as a prepositional/locative particle in poetry ('at/on'); موبدان is the plural of موبد (priests); داشتی is the 2nd‑person past form 'you had' (poetic address to the king or addressee). The clause literally: 'At court you had mobeds.'
segments.s653-g182: ز هر دانشی بخردان داشتی: ز = 'from' or 'of' (poetic genitive marker), هر دانشی = 'every kind/branch of knowledge', بخردان is the plural agentive/adjective 'the wise ones' (بخرد = wise persons), and داشتی again is 'you had.' The line means you possessed wise men versed in every science. Word order (from‑phrase followed by possessed noun) is typical of poetic compression.
segments.s653-g183: همیشه سخن گوی هفتاد مرد is a poetic noun phrase: همیشه 'always' modifies the whole group; سخن گوی (a compound meaning 'speech‑speaker' / 'orator') describes the function of the men; هفتاد مرد is the numeral + noun 'seventy men.' In Persian poetic order the modifier (سخن گوی) can precede the numeral; the sense is 'always seventy men who were speakers.'
segments.s653-g184: به درگاه بودی بخواب و بخورد: به درگاه 'at the court' (به = 'at/to', درگاه = 'royal court/presence'); بودی is the past 2nd‑person singular of 'to be' (here used impersonally or addressing the addressee: 'were' / 'you had'); بخواب و بخورد are infinitives 'to sleep and to eat' (بخواب here functions as the infinitival form 'to sleep' rather than an imperative). The clause gives the purpose/condition of those men (they were at court for eating and sleeping).
segments.s653-g377: بداد و ستد appears as a compact phrase referring to the condition ('oppression' / wrongdoing) and the action around it; وستد here is a third‑person verb root meaning 'seize' or 'take'. در is the preposition 'in/into', کند is a third‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do' ('he makes/puts'), and راستی is the direct object meaning 'uprightness' or 'rightness'. The phrase literally orders: 'he makes uprightness into (the place of) oppression.'
segments.s653-g378: ببندد is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form of بستن 'to close' (here 'he closes' or 'he will shut'); در is used as a noun 'door/gate' (the thing being closed); کژی and کاستی are nouns meaning 'crookedness' and 'deficiency' respectively, coordinated by و. The clause means 'he closes the door on crookedness and deficiency.'
segments.s653-g379: ببخشد is a 3rd‑person form of بخشیدن and here functions as 'he forgives' (rather than 'gives'); گنه is the object 'sin/fault'. چون + شود + کامکار forms a subordinate temporal/conditional clause 'when/if he becomes prosperous' (چون acting as a conjunction and شود as 'becomes').
segments.s653-g380: نباشد is the negative copula 'is not' (classical form), placed before the predicate; سرش is 'his head' with the enclitic ش marking possession; تیز and بردبار are opposing predicate adjectives 'sharp/quick' and 'patient/forbearing', joined by و, with نا negating the second adjective to yield 'neither... nor...' The clause reads 'his head is not sharp and not (i.e. nor) patient,' i.e. 'his temper is neither hasty nor patient.'
segments.s653-g381: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'he asked' (3rd person). دیگر here means 'further/another' (i.e. he asked another question). که introduces the embedded question/clause 'which (of them)...'; از is the preposition 'of/from/concerning' and انجمن is the noun 'assembly'—together they form 'which (one) of the assembly'.
segments.s653-g382: نگهبان is the predicate noun 'guardian/protector'; کدامست is the interrogative copula 'which is it?' (کدام + است fused), asking to identify which person; برخویشتن is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'with respect to oneself' or 'self‑possession'—so the clause asks 'which (one) is guardian of himself (i.e. self‑restrained).'
